name: Present Tense (-are verbs)
summary: Conjugate regular -are verbs, the largest and most useful verb group in Italian.
explanation:
Most Italian verbs end in -are (parlare, to speak; mangiare, to eat). To conjugate them, drop the -are to get the stem (parl-) and add the present-tense endings.

The endings are: -o (io), -i (tu), -a (lui/lei), -iamo (noi), -ate (voi), -ano (loro). Because the endings are so distinct, Italian usually drops the subject pronoun: parlo already means 'I speak'.

The Italian present covers both 'I speak' and 'I am speaking', so parlo means both.
pattern: stem + o / i / a / iamo / ate / ano
commonMistakes: Stressing the wrong syllable in loro forms. It is PARlano, not parLAno.
Adding an English continuous form. There is no 'io sono parlare'; just say parlo.
Confusing the tu ending -i with the noi ending -iamo.
Keeping the subject pronoun everywhere. Italian usually drops io, tu, lui.
Forgetting that verbs in -iare (mangiare) keep only one i in the tu form: mangi, not mangii.
tip: Chant the endings: o, i, a, iamo, ate, ano. They fit thousands of -are verbs.
